coredata.dc:title: Deep eutectic solvents-derivated carbon dots-decorated silica stationary phase with enhanced separation selectivity in reversed-phase liquid chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Gafang
Chen, Jia
Qiu, Hongdeng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, deep eutectic solvents-based carbon dots (DESCDs) were prepared and bonded to the silica surface for the first time to form a new hydrophobic chromatographic stationary phase (Sil-DESCDs). The successful preparation of DESCDs and Sil-DESCDs were demonstrated by a series of characterizations including transmission electron microscopies, laser scanning confocal microscope, Fourier transform infrared spectrometry, elemental analysis, etc. Retention behavior of Sil-DESCDs was evaluated using Tanaka and Engelhardt standard test mixtures. The results showed that this new stationary phase had excellent separation performance for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, flavonoids, aromatic amines and phenolic compounds. Excellent separation selectivity for the 3-phenylene ring isomers including phenanthrene and anthracene, the 4-phenylene ring isomers including pyrene, triphenylene, chrysene and 1,2-benzanthracene was also obtained. Especially, prednisolone and hydrocortisone, which have very similar structures, can be separated using pure water as the mobile phase. In addition, the flavonoids in Astragalus extracts including calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin and formononetin were determined using this new column, their concentrations were 0.050, 0.031, 0.023 and 0.034 mg/mL, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Deep eutectic solvents
Stationary phase
Reverse-phase liquid chromatography
Chromatographic separation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tanaka and Engelhardt standard test mixture
Separation of PAHs, aromatic amines and phenols
Separation of prednisolone, hydrocortisone and isomers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations: TEM image of DESCDs (a); Diameter distribution statistics of DESCDs (b); EDX spectrum of DESCDs (c); LSCM image of Sil-DESCDs (d); Zeta potential of DESCDs, Sil-APS and Sil-DESCDs (e); FT-IR spectra of DESCDs, Sil-APS, Silica and Sil-DESCDs (f).

Effect of acetonitrile concentration (a) and column temperature (b) on the retention factor (k) of five alkylbenzenes on the Sil-DESCDs column. Analytes: (1) toluene, (2) ethylbenzene, (3) n-propylbenzene, (4) n-butylbenzene, (5) n-pentylbenzene. Mobile phase: (a) (40%−60%) acetonitrile-water, (b) acetonitrile-water (50/50, v/v). Column temperature: (a) 30 °C, (b) 25–45 °C. Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1. UV detection wavelength: 254 nm.

Comparisons of Sil-DESCDs and ZORBAX SB-C18 columns for the separation of the PAHs (a, d), aromatic amines (b, e) and phenols (c, f). PAHs: (1) anthrone, (2) naphthalene, (3) 2-methylnaphthalene, (4) acenaphthene, (5) fluorene, (6) phenanthrene, (7) anthracene, (8) pyrene, (9) triphenylene, (10) chrysene, (11) 1,2-benzoanthracene. Aromatic amines: (1) p-phenylenediamine, (2) p-toluidine, (3) N-methylaniline, (4) m-nitroaniline, (5) N,N-dimethylaniline, (6) 4-chloroacetanilide, (7) methylnaphthylamine, (8) β-naphthylamine, (9) 2,4-dichloroaniline, (10) 2,6-diisopropylphenylamine, (11) diphenylamine, (12) 2,4,6-tribromoaniline. Phenols: (1) p-aminophenol, (2) m-aminophenol, (3) 2-aminophenol, (4) phenol, (5) 3,5-dimethylphenol, (6) p-chlorophenol, (7) p‑tert-butylphenol. Mobile phase: (a) acetonitrile-water (28/72, v/v), (b) acetonitrile-water (16/84, v/v), (c) acetonitrile-water (15/85, v/v), (d) acetonitrile-water (60/40, v/v), (e) acetonitrile-water (45/55, v/v), (f) acetonitrile-water (40/60, v/v). Column temperature: 30 °C. Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1. UV detection wavelength: 254 nm.

Comparison of the separation of hydrocortisone and prednisolone on Sil-DESCDs (a, b), Sil-APS (c) and ZORBAX SB-C18 (d) columns. Analytes: (1) hydrocortisone, (2) prednisolone. Mobile phase: (a) acetonitrile-water (7/93, v/v), (b) water (100, v), (c) acetonitrile-water (7/93, v/v), (d) acetonitrile-water (25/75, v/v). Column temperature: 30 °C. Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1. UV detection wavelength: 254 nm.

(a) Chromatograms of the separation of calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin, formononetin and actual sample. (b) Standard curve of calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin, formononetin. Mobile phase: acetonitrile-0.1% formic acid aqueous solution (22/78, v/v). Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1. UV detection wavelength: 254 nm.

The stability test of Sil-DESCDs column. Analytes: (1) calycosin-7-glucoside, (2) ononin, (3) astragalin, (4) calycosin, (5) formononetin, (6) genistein. Chromatographic conditions are the same as Fig. 6a.

head.ce:title: Deep eutectic solvents-derivated carbon dots-decorated silica stationary phase with enhanced separation selectivity in reversed-phase liquid chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory on Pollution Monitoring and Control, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi 830054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory on Pollution Monitoring and Control
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Gannan Normal University, Ganzhou 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCollege of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Gannan Normal University, Ganzhou 341000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, deep eutectic solvents-based carbon dots (DESCDs) were prepared and bonded to the silica surface for the first time to form a new hydrophobic chromatographic stationary phase (Sil-DESCDs). The successful preparation of DESCDs and Sil-DESCDs were demonstrated by a series of characterizations including transmission electron microscopies, laser scanning confocal microscope, Fourier transform infrared spectrometry, elemental analysis, etc. Retention behavior of Sil-DESCDs was evaluated using Tanaka and Engelhardt standard test mixtures. The results showed that this new stationary phase had excellent separation performance for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, flavonoids, aromatic amines and phenolic compounds. Excellent separation selectivity for the 3-phenylene ring isomers including phenanthrene and anthracene, the 4-phenylene ring isomers including pyrene, triphenylene, chrysene and 1,2-benzanthracene was also obtained. Especially, prednisolone and hydrocortisone, which have very similar structures, can be separated using pure water as the mobile phase. In addition, the flavonoids in Astragalus extracts including calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin and formononetin were determined using this new column, their concentrations were 0.050, 0.031, 0.023 and 0.034 mg/mL, respectively.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing development of science and technology, a number of technologies of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) are constantly innovating and developing. Chromatographic columns are the core of chromatographic systems, and the innovation of the column is mainly focused on the preparation of high-performance chromatographic packing materials to cope with the separation of complex samples [1–3]. Silica is the most popular stationary phase matrix for HPLC because of its structural stability, high column efficiency, easy surface modification and high mechanical strength and other characteristics [4–6]. Recently, polymers [7], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [8], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [9], ionic liquids [10], and carbon materials [11,12] are used to modify silica surface to improve the chromatographic properties.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new type of carbon nanomaterials with particle size less than 10 nm, which have the merits of simple preparation process, sufficient carbon source, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, stable photophysical and chemical properties, etc. [13–18] Since CDs were first reported in 2004, they have been extensively used in sensing [19], catalysis [20], biomedical [21], environmental [22] and other fields. Comparing with other members of the carbon family, CDs are known for their nanometer size, abundant surface functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino groups), high stability, and moderate adsorption capacity, which can avoid agglomeration like other carbon materials when used as chromatographic stationary phases and uniformly modify to the silica surface, and has a bright application prospect in the chromatographic field [23]. Our group prepared a series of hydrophilic CDs using amino acids, polyethyleneimine, imidazolium ionic liquids and glucose as carbon sources, and bonded the CDs to the silica surface by the "Nano-On-Micro" strategy to prepare a series of hydrophilic chromatographic packing materials, which showed excellent separation performance for nucleoside bases, ginsenosides, sugars, amino acids and antibiotics, etc [24–26]. Hydrophobic CDs were also prepared using octadecylamine and octadecylimidazolium ionic liquids as carbon sources, respectively, and these CDs were bonded to the silica surface by silylation reagents as a bridge, a series of hydrophobic chromatographic stationary phases were prepared, which had certain separation properties for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), phenols, alkylbenzenes and sulfonamides [27,28].

Deep eutectic solvents (DESs) is formed by the interaction between hydrogen bond donor and hydrogen bond acceptor, which have received increasing attention since their discovery in 2003 [29], due to their environmental friendliness, high biocompatibility, easy biodegradability, low cost, and simple preparation methods, etc [30–33]. They are widely used in sample pre-treatment [34], material preparation [35], electrochemistry, etc [36]. DESs as a new generation of green solvents, also have a wide range of applications in the field of chromatography, for example, replacing organic solvents in the preparation of stationary phases as reaction solvents, as mobile phases or additives to mobile phases, as materials for chromatographic stationary phases, etc. [37,38] As far as we know, the study of DESs based CDs bonded silica for HPLC stationary phase has not been reported. Moreover, when DESs are used for CDs, some DESs can introduce heteroatoms, which can add richer components to the preparation of CDs [39].

Therefore, in this study, DESs-based CDs (DESCDs) were prepared by solvothermal method using choline chloride (ChCl)/lactic acid (LA) type DESs as carbon source, and a new stationary phase (Sil-DESCDs) were prepared by chemically modifying DESCDs onto the silica surface. The successful grafting of DESCDs was confirmed by scanning electron microscopy, laser confocal microscopy, zeta potential, FT-IR spectroscopy, elemental analysis, etc. Enhanced hydrophobic chromatographic selectivity can be found when used for the separation of alkylbenzenes, PAHs, aromatic amines, phenolic compounds, etc. Interestingly, prednisolone and hydrocortisone, which is difficult to be separated in commercial column because of their similar structures, can be excellently separated in this new stationary phase using pure water as the mobile phase. Finally, the Sil-DESCDs column was also applied to the qualitative and quantitative analysis of flavonoids in Astragalus herb extracts.

All materials and reagents were used directly without purification. Silica (diameter: 3 µm) was supported from Shanxi Intelligent Micro Technology Company, Limited (IMTECH). (3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APS), diethyl pyrocarbonate (DEPC), triethylamine (TEA) and chromatographic standard samples (PAHs, alkylbenzenes, aromatic amines, phenolic compounds) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Company, Limited (Shanghai, China). Flavonoids (calycosin-7-glucoside, calycosin, ononin, formononetin, astragalin, genistein) were purchased from Chengdu Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Dialysis bag (500 Da) was bought in Beijing Solarbao Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The commercial ZORBAX SB-C18 column (150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d.) was provided by Agilent Technologies (California, USA). Other chemicals and solvents were purchased from Energy Chemicals (Shanghai, China).

Chromatographic separation was performed by using an EasySep-3030 HPLC system (Unimicro Technologies, Inc., Shanghai). All stationary phases were packed on a 95,551 U HPLC column loading machine (Alltech, Germany). Transmission electron microscopies (TEM) imaging were obtained from Tecnai G2 TF20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) imaging were performed on Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope FV1200 (Olympus, Japan). Contact angle measurement experimental data from Dataphysics OCA 20 contact angle measurement instrument (Filderstadt, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared spectrum results were collected from IFS 120HR Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Bruker, Germany). Elemental analysis results were measured on a Vario EL III element analyzer (Hanau, Germany).
As shown in Fig. 1 , the preparation of the new stationary phase includes the following three steps.
ChCl and LA were mixed (molar ratio of ChCl/LA=1:3) with magnetic stirring for 1.5 h at 80 °C until a uniform and transparent solution was formed, the produced solution is defined as DESs [40].

The DESs was added to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h [39]. After the reaction was completed and cooled to room temperature, the product was put into cellulose ester membrane (500 Da) and dialyzed against water for 24 h. The dialyzed solution was filtered through 0.22 µm membrane and concentrated by rotary evaporation, and the concentrated solution was frozen in a refrigerator for 24 h. Finally, the solids were freeze-dried and the DESs-based CDs named DESCDs were obtained.

3.0 g of silica was uniformly dispersed in 20 mL of toluene and 1.5 mL of APS was added into the mixed solution. Then, the mixed solution was mechanically stirred and refluxed at 110 °C for 24 h. The product was centrifuged and sequentially washed several times with toluene, ethanol, ethanol/water (50/50, v/v) and methanol. Finally, the product was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain aminopropyl-modified silica (Sil-APS).

3.0 g of Sil-APS and 0.8 g of DESCDs were dispersed into 20 mL of toluene, 1.5 g of DEPC and 1.1 g of TEA were added as the catalyst, and the mixture was mechanically stirred and refluxed at 75 °C for 24 h [41]. After the reaction was completed, the product was centrifuged and washed several times with toluene, ethanol and methanol in order, and finally dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain Sil-DESCDs.

Firstly, 1.7 g of Sil-DESCDs and Sil-APS stationary phases were dispersed into 56 mL of methanol, respectively, and sonicated for 10 min to form a suspension. Then, the suspension was punched into a stainless-steel column (100 mm × 4.6 mm I.D.) at 40 MPa with methanol as the topping solution.

3.0 g of Astragalus powder (passed through 20 mesh sieve) was added to 30 mL of methanol and then ultrasonic extraction for 2 h at room temperature, then left to stand overnight. The supernatant was filtered through 0.22 µm filter membrane.

A series of characterizations demonstrated the successful preparation of Sil-DESCDs. The morphology and size of DESCDs can be observed by TEM, it can be seen from the Fig. 2 a that DESCDs show quasi-spherical morphology, and their average size was about 3.23 nm according to the diameter distribution statistics (Fig. 2b). The energy spectrum illustrated that the DESCDs contain C, N, O and Cl elements (Fig. 2c). The bright fluorescence of Sil-DESCDs can be seen by LSCM imaging, indicating that DESCDs are successfully bonded to the silica surface (Fig. 2d). The zeta potential results of DESCDs, Sil-APS and Sil-DESCDs show that they are all positively charged, and the positive charge of Sil-DESCDs is larger than that of Sil-APS, indicating that the modification of DESCDs to the surface of Sil-APS increased the positive charge of its surface (Fig. 2e). The results of elemental analysis showed that the N%, C% and H% content of Sil-DESCDs (2.68%, 10.99% and 2.22%) was significantly increased compared to that of Sil-APS (2.08%, 6.55% and 1.64%), this indicates that DESCDs are successfully bonded to the silica surface (Table S1). The hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of Sil-DESCDs and Sil-APS materials were verified by contact angle experiments, the value of θ for Sil-APS is 87°, as can be seen from the Fig. S1, which indicates that the surface of Sil-APS is hydrophilic (θ<90°), while the θ value of 137.7° for Sil-DESCDs indicates that Sil-DESCDs are hydrophobic (θ>90°). The results of contact angle by Sil-APS and Sil-DESCDs also illustrate the successful grafting of DESCDs on the silica surface.

The FT-IR spectra of Sil-DESCDs, Sil-APS, Silica and DESCDs are shown in Fig. 2f. The absorption peaks at 3342 cm−1 and 3415 cm−1 are attributed to the OH absorption peaks on DESCDs and Sil-DESCDs. The absorption peak at 3383 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration absorption peak of NH on Sil-APS. 1742 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic peak of C = O in the spectrum of DESCDs, and the C = O absorption peak of Sil-DESCDs appears at 1722 cm−1. The appearance of new absorption peaks at 3415 cm−1 and 1722 cm−1 in the spectra of Sil-DESCDs compared to Sil-APS, that indicates the successful modification of DESCDs on the silica surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tanaka and Engelhardt standard test mixture
ce:section.ce:para: The chromatograms of the Tanaka standard test mixture on the Sil-DESCDs and the ZORBAX SB-C18 columns is as shown in Fig. S2 (a, d). The retention time of uracil was used as the dead time. The ratio of the retention factors of caffeine and phenol is 3.61, which indicates that the Sil-DESCDs column has some hydrogen bonding ability, the ratio of the retention factors of n-pentylbenzene and n-butylbenzene was 1.66, indicating that the Sil-DESCDs column has good hydrophobic selectivity (Table S2). O-terphenyl and triphenylene have similar hydrophobicity, while their spatial structures are different, therefore, the retention factor ratio of both can be used to evaluate the shape selectivity of the column, the ratio of retention factors of triphenylene and o-terphenyl on Sil-DESCDs column was 1.79 (Table S2), suggesting that Sil-DESCDs columns have certain shape selectivity.

Engelhardt standard test mixture consists of thiourea, aniline, p-toluidine, phenol, ethylbenzene and toluene, which is utilized for further evaluation of stationary phases. The chromatograms of Engelhardt standard test species on Sil-DESCDs and ZORBAX SB-C18 columns also can be shown in Fig. S2 (b, e). Retention time of thiourea was used as dead time. The ratio of the retention factors of aniline and phenol is 1.89 (Table S3), and the elution order of both was aniline and phenol, indicating that the Sil-DESCDs column had lower silanol activity. Ethylbenzene and toluene are hydrophobic substances, the retention factor ratio of both can be used to evaluate the hydrophobicity of the column, the ratio of retention factors of ethylbenzene and toluene on the Sil-DESCDs column is 1.69 (Table S3), which indicating that the stationary phase of Sil-DESCDs has hydrophobic selectivity.

As shown in the Fig. S2 (c, f), the elution order of the five alkylbenzenes on the Sil-DESCDs column was toluene, ethylbenzene, n-propylbenzene, n-butylbenzene and n-pentylbenzene, which is consistent with that of the ZORBAX SB-C18 column, suggesting that the Sil-DESCDs column is hydrophobic, and the retention times of the five alkylbenzenes on the ZORBAX SB-C18 column were longer under the same chromatographic conditions, this indicates that the hydrophobicity of Sil-DESCDs column is weaker than that of ZORBAX SB-C18 column.

The effect of acetonitrile content on the retention of alkylbenzenes was further investigated. As shown in Fig.S3a and Fig. 3 a, the retention factors of all five alkylbenzenes decreased as increasing acetonitrile from 40% to 60% and this phenomenon exhibited a reverse-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) mechanism, indicating that the Sil-DESCDs column is hydrophobic, under the same acetonitrile ratio condition, the retention factor of alkylbenzene increased with the increasing of alkyl chain length, indicating that the Sil-DESCDs column has some hydrophobic selectivity. These also illustrates the simultaneous existence of hydrophobic interactions between alkylbenzenes and stationary phases in the RPLC mode. The column temperature also has an effect on the retention of analytes. Therefore, the relationship between the retention time of five alkylbenzenes and the column temperature was investigated from 25 °C to 45 °C (Fig. S3b). As shown in Fig. 3b, the retention factors of all five alkylbenzenes decreased with increasing temperature, the Gibbs free energy change (ΔG), enthalpy change (ΔH) and entropy change (ΔS) in this process species are calculated using the Van't Hoff equation. As shown in Table S4, the ΔG of all five alkylbenzenes decreased gradually as the column temperature increased, indicating that the retention of the five alkylbenzenes on the Sil-DESCDs column was more beneficial at lower temperatures. The negative value of ΔH indicates that the retention of the five alkylbenzenes on the Sil-DESCDs column is an exothermic process. The different adsorption interactions of the each five alkylbenzenes in the retention mechanism leading to different slopes in the plots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of PAHs, aromatic amines and phenols
ce:section.ce:para: The carbonyl groups in LA can be worked as an electron acceptor group for PAHs and generate π-π interactions. Therefore, Sil-DESCDs column has a strong ability to identify PAHs [42]. The chromatograms of eleven PAHs on Sil-DESCDs and ZORBAX SB-C18 columns are shown in Fig. 4 (a, d). The separation of the eleven PAHs is well achieved on the Sil-DESCDs column within 60 min. The three benzene ring isomers (phenanthrene and anthracene), the four benzene ring isomers (pyrene, triphenylene, chrysene and 1,2-benzanthracene) were well separated on Sil-DESCDs column, we know that the separation of two pairs of isomers, phenanthrene and anthracene, chrysene and 1,2-benzanthracene usually is difficult in the commercially used C18 column. The resolution of phenanthrene and anthracene on the Sil-DESCDs column is 2.2 and the resolution of chrysene and 1,2-benzanthracene on the Sil-DESCDs column is 1.9. The effect of acetonitrile content on the retention of the eleven PAHs is further studied in Fig. S4, the retention factors of all eleven PAHs decreased gradually with increasing the proportion of acetonitrile content, this suggests that the retention of PAHs on the Sil-DESCDs column is caused by the synergistic effect of both hydrophobic and π-π interactions between PAHs and Sil-DESCDs column.

The chromatographic performance of the Sil-DESCDs column was further evaluated using basic (aromatic amines, Fig. 4b) and acidic compounds (phenols, Fig. 4c)). Some aromatic amines are considered to be carcinogenic substances, and when the human body is exposed to them for a long time, such substances will combine with some macromolecules such as proteins in the human body to generate carcinogenic substances and cause lesions in the body, so testing for aromatic amines is necessary [43]. The chromatograms of the twelve aromatic amines on Sil-DESCDs and ZORBAX SB-C18 columns are shown in Fig. 4b and Fig. 4e, good separation of twelve aromatic amines was achieved on Sil-DESCDs column, however, the elution order of individual aromatic amines on the ZORBAX SB-C18 column is different from that of the Sil-DESCDs column, which might be due to the formation of new forces after the bonding of DESCDs on silica. The seven phenolic compounds eluted in the same order on the Sil-DESCDs and the ZORBAX SB-C18 columns. Compared Fig. 4c and Fig. 4f, we can see better separation was obtained on the Sil-DESCDs column. The separation of the seven phenolic compounds was achieved by hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions between the phenolic compounds and the Sil-DESCDs column.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of prednisolone, hydrocortisone and isomers
ce:section.ce:para: The separation of structurally similar steroidal compounds has been a challenge for HPLC, and different stationary phases were prepared to distinguish these compound [44,45]. Prednisolone and hydrocortisone are two steroidal compounds with very similar structures, the difference is only in the double bond at the C-1 position, the commercially available C30 stationary phase showed excellent separation of prednisolone and hydrocortisone [46]. Fig. 5 shows the chromatographic separation of hydrocortisone and prednisolone on Sil-DESCDs, Sil-APS and ZORBAX SB-C18 columns. We can see that the separation of prednisolone and hydrocortisone could not be achieved on the Sil-APS column (Fig. 5c) and ZORBAX SB-C18 column (Fig. 5d), but could be well achieved on the Sil-DESCDs column (Fig. 5a), which further indicates that the DESCDs were successfully bonded to the silica and the addition of DESCDs improved the separation performance of the Sil-DESCDs column. Reducing the use of organic solvents and using pure water as the mobile phase is in line with the development of "green" chemistry, excellent baseline separation of prednisolone and hydrocortisone was also achieved on the Sil-DESCDs column with a resolution of 2.6 under the condition of pure water as mobile phase (Fig. 5b).

Sil-DESCDs column has also been used to separate isomers, three groups of isomers p-aminophenol, m-aminophenol and o-aminophenol (Fig. S5a), p-dihydroxybenzene, m-dihydroxybenzene and o-dihydroxybenzene (Fig. S5b), p-nitroaniline, m-nitroaniline and o-nitroaniline (Fig. S5c) were well separated in Sil-DESCDs column, indicating that our column has a good application prospect in the field of isomer separation.

Flavonoids are widely found in many plants, and HPLC is also a common method for the quantitative and qualitative analysis of flavonoids. The chromatograms of the six flavonoids on Sil-DESCDs and ZORBAX SB-C18 columns are shown in Fig. S6a and Fig. S6b. In addition, the flavonoids in Astragalus extracts including calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin and formononetin were detected by using Sil-DESCDs column (Fig. 6 a), and their concentration of the extracted contents were calculated from the standard curves in as 0.050, 0.031, 0.023 and 0.034 mg/mL, respectively (Fig. 6b).

The stability of Sil-DESCDs column was investigated systematically by using 6 flavonoids as test samples and injecting them 10 times continuously under the same chromatographic conditions. As shown in Fig. 7 , the relative standard deviation (RSD) values of the retention times of the six flavonoids in the Sil-DESCDs column ranged from 0.5% to 1.3% (n = 10), indicating that the stationary phase of Sil-DESCDs has good stability.

In conclusion, DESs-based CDs were prepared and grafted on silica packing as new RPLC stationary phases. Sil-DESCDs stationary phase exhibited good hydrophobicity, π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding, which enabled the highly selective separation of 11 PAHs, 12 aromatic amines, 7 phenols and 6 flavonoids. In addition, Sil-DESCDs column provides a new way to separate the structural analogs such as prednisolone and hydrocortisone, which can be well separated under the conditions of pure water as the mobile phase. This work demonstrated that CDs-modified silica materials is potential to be used in the future.

Gafang Fu: Formal analysis, Software, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jia Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hongdeng Qiu: Visualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has received financial support from Longyuan Youth Innovation and Entrepreneurship Talent (Team) Project (No. E20492SC), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074154), Lanzhou talent innovation and Entrepreneurship Project (No. 2021-RC-35), Science and Technology Project of Science and Technology Bureau of Chengguan District in Lanzhou (No. 2020JSCX0033), Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (No. 2021420).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chroma.2022.463425.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dendritic mesoporous silica nanospheres: toward the ultimate minimum particle size for ultraefficient liquid chromatographic separation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Guo, Y. Chen, S. Wang, J. Pang, Z. Liu, Dendritic mesoporous silica nanospheres: toward the ultimate minimum particle size for ultraefficient liquid chromatographic separation, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter.13 (2021) 22970–22977.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ growth of COF-rLZU1 on the surface of silica sphere as stationary phase for high performance liquid chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xu, Z. Li, L. Zhang, W. Zhang, D. Li, In situ growth of COF-rLZU1 on the surface of silica sphere as stationary phase for high performance liquid chromatography, Talanta221 (2021) 121612.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of a poly(ethyleneimine) embedded phenyl stationary phase for mixed-mode liquid chromatography
